coredata.dc:title: Photoactivatable carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for picric acid detection and light-induced bacterial inactivation

coredata.dc:description: The zero-dimensional carbon nanostructure known as carbon dots showed attractive attributes such as multicolour emission, very high quantum yield, up-conversion, very good aqueous solubility, eco-friendliness, and excellent biocompatibility. These outstanding features of the carbon dots have raised significant interest among the research community worldwide. In the current work, water-soluble nitrogen, silver, and gold co-doped bimetallic carbon dots (BCDs) were prepared using the one-pot hydrothermal method with citric acid as a sole carbon source. As prepared BCDs showed size in the range of 4–8 nm and excitation-independent emission behaviour with maximum emission observed at 427 nm. Additionally, these BCDs showed a very high quantum yield value of 50% and fluorescence lifetime value of 10.1 ns respectively. Interestingly, as prepared BCDs selectively sense picric acid (PA) by exhibiting “selective fluorescence turn-off” behaviour in the presence of PA with a limit of detection value (LOD) of 46 nM. Further, as prepared BCDs were explored for photodynamic therapy to inactivate bacterial growth in the presence of light (400–700 nm) by generating singlet oxygen. Thus as prepared BCDs offer lots of potentials to use a nanoprobe to detect picric acid in an aqueous medium and to design next-generation antibacterial materials.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The zero-dimensional carbon nanostructure known as carbon dots showed attractive attributes such as multicolour emission, very high quantum yield, up-conversion, very good aqueous solubility, eco-friendliness, and excellent biocompatibility. These outstanding features of the carbon dots have raised significant interest among the research community worldwide. In the current work, water-soluble nitrogen, silver, and gold co-doped bimetallic carbon dots (BCDs) were prepared using the one-pot hydrothermal method with citric acid as a sole carbon source. As prepared BCDs showed size in the range of 4–8 nm and excitation-independent emission behaviour with maximum emission observed at 427 nm. Additionally, these BCDs showed a very high quantum yield value of 50% and fluorescence lifetime value of 10.1 ns respectively. Interestingly, as prepared BCDs selectively sense picric acid (PA) by exhibiting “selective fluorescence turn-off” behaviour in the presence of PA with a limit of detection value (LOD) of 46 nM. Further, as prepared BCDs were explored for photodynamic therapy to inactivate bacterial growth in the presence of light (400–700 nm) by generating singlet oxygen. Thus as prepared BCDs offer lots of potentials to use a nanoprobe to detect picric acid in an aqueous medium and to design next-generation antibacterial materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitroaromatic explosives, which have been used more in recent years are hazardous for humans, animals as well as environment [1]. Among these nitroaromatic compounds, picric acid is highly water-soluble, the fact which makes it stand out as the most hazardous environment pollutant [2]. So, the rapid and accurate detection of highly water-soluble picric acid is an important field of current research. The detection methods and techniques along with the newly developed sensors for picric acid determination have been explored using various fluorescent probes recently [3–10]. The excellent water solubility of picric acid is the main concern that makes it dangerous water and soil contaminant which can cause adverse effects on human and animal health [11–13]. Therefore, the determination of picric acid in an aqueous medium has gained substantial effort from the research community lately. Among the various other materials as picric acid sensors, the carbon dots attracted immense interest from researchers due to their commendatory performance. Many properties of carbon dots distinguish them from others like appealing optical properties, excellent water solubility, high biocompatibility, easy surface passivation, and environment-friendliness [14–19]. The carbon dots-based sensors had been used for sensing a wide range of analytes such as metal ions [20,21], biomolecules [22–25], drugs [26–28], and many more [29–34].

Healthy living is a prime concern in today's life, so the materials that have the potential of killing injurious bacteria and other microorganisms are gaining interest rapidly. Coating of aforesaid materials is done on the surfaces of day to day used items which are frequently touched by several individuals like doorbells, public swings, toys, switchboards, phones to make them antimicrobial so that the transmission of infectious diseases could be controlled [35–37]. Improper use of antibiotics is a leading cause of antibiotic resistance in many cases, so off-the-centred plans of action are required to gear up for such infections [38,39]. Recently, photodynamic therapy for treating cancer and infectious diseases is gaining interest. Photodynamic antimicrobial therapy (PDAMT) is appropriate for treating surface microbial infections such as skin infection, burn tissues infection, bedsores in elderly patients, acne, and for making aseptic surfaces [40,41]. The materials which could act as photosensitizers in photodynamic antimicrobial therapy are the materials of choice. Moreover, the chances of developing resistant strains are almost nil in this PDAMT in contrast to conventional antibiotic therapies. Conventionally in PDAMT, UV or NIR lights are used to irradiate photo-sensitizers for the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), for bactericidal action. UV and NIR light exposure are hazardous to humans, so visible light activatable agents have been presented with extra attention of late. Various new visible light activatable agents such as gold nano-particles, ruthenium complexes, TiO2 nanoparticles have been synthesized in recent years [42,43]. Surface-modified carbon dots have been showcased as efficient visible light-activated antimicrobial agents [44–47].

Given this, present work focused on the synthesis of nitrogen, silver, and gold bimetallic heteroatom doped carbon dots (BCDs) using citric acid as the sole carbon source having excellent quantum yield and greater aqueous solubility for the label-free detection of picric acid in an aqueous system. As synthesized BCDs showed commendable sensitivity, selectivity, and precision towards picric acid detection. Additionally, these BCDs were also explored as a photosensitizer for efficient bacterial elimination owing to their visible light activatable nature which can be used to fabricate advanced biocidal surface coatings to keep surfaces free from any potential pathogenic microbes.

All the analytical reagent grade chemicals, used for the preparation of carbon dots as well as for sensing were procured from Merck and Sigma-Aldrich. No further purification was done. Deionized water (DI) of the Sartorius milli-Q system was used for all the experiments.

Rigaku, RINT smart lab 2500 V X-ray diffractometer was used for performing X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (1.5406 Å). The UV–visible spectroscopic studies were performed using Varian Cary 100 Bio UV–visible spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectrophotometry was carried out using a Fluoromax spectrofluorometer. Bio-Rad FTS 3000MX instrument was used for recording IR spectra (4000–400 cm−1). FEI Tecnai G2- F20 Transmission Electron Microscope was used for capturing TEM images. Elemental mapping was done on an EDAX Octane Elite TW 55 Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) instrument. Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) data were recorded for fluorescence lifetime studies using the TCSPC system model-Fluorocube-01-NL. The adsorption was recorded for MTT assay using Synergy H1 Biotek microplate reader. LabRAM HR (UV system) was used for Raman spectroscopy measurement using a 785 nm laser source. Thermo-scientific NEXA Surface analyser and Nexion 2000B ICP-MS (Perkin Elmer) were used to record X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) and ICP-OES respectively.

Bimetallic carbon dots were synthesized through the traditional hydrothermal method. First of all, 4 g citric acid was added to 20 mL of DI water. In the next step, all the other chemicals (28 mg of HAuCl4, 10 mg of AgNO3, 500 mg Bovine serum albumin, and 80 mg of Urea) were weighed and added to the aqueous solution of citric acid. Sonication of the mixture was done for 10 mins for homogenous dispersion of all the ingredients. The solution so formed was poured into a Teflon coated autoclave. The autoclave was kept in the furnace at 180 °C for 4 h for hydrothermal carbonization. After cooling at room temperature, the final suspension was centrifuged which helped in the removal of any solid remains. The remaining clear solution was lyophilized. The sticky lyophilized product was weighed and re-suspended to form a yellow-coloured solution using DI water for further use.

The procedure is recommended in the manual by HORIBA Jobin Yvon IBH Ltd. “A Guide to Recording Fluorescence Quantum Yields” [48] was referred to for calculating quantum yield (QY). Following eq. (1) was used for the calculation of fluorescence quantum yield (ΦF) of BCDs. 0.1 M H2SO4 was used to dissolve quinine sulfate to be used as standard with quantum yield 0.54 [49,50].

ΦF and Φst are the quantum yield of the sample and standard respectively, the absorbance of the standard and sample are ascribed to Ast and As at their respective excitation wavelength. The integrated fluorescence intensity was plotted against the absorbance of the standard for calculating the slope of standard Sst and the slope of sample Ss at various divergent concentrations. The refractive index of solvent for standard and the sample were represented as nst and ns respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol (Picric Acid) and Selectivity Studies
ce:section.ce:para:
A dispersed aqueous solution of BCDs was prepared according to a typical procedure to be used for photoluminescence (PL) studies. Further, in a 1 cm quartz cuvette, 3 mL of the prepared aqueous solution was poured to record the fluorescence response in the range of 370–730 nm upon 360 nm excitation. The maximum attained intensity was set as control (F0). Nitroaromatics solution (10 mM) were prepared afresh for sensing studies. The spectra of BCDs solution after the addition of different nitroaromatic were recorded at λex. 360 nm excitation. The experimental conditions were kept the same for all the studies. Fluorescence response kinetics of BCDs were studied using lifetime decay data recorded at TCSPC system and analyzed using IBH DAS 6.0 software [22]. The reduced χ-square (χ2) value was used to investigate fit, value, and fluorescence lifetime decay was measured by using the iterative reconciliation method. The decay values were fitted with a three exponential function by utilizing equation no. 2.
(2)

Here, F(τ) represents the fluorescence decay (normalized), α denotes the amplitude of the decay component τ. Whereas, the average lifetime was calculated by utilizing equation no. 3.
(3)

Σα i
τ i

Biocompatibility of BCDs was checked using MCF7 (Breast cancer cell line) via MTT assay using (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazole-2-yl)-2,5- diphenyltetrazolium bromide) [51,52]. Live cells were quantified by colorimetric analysis. The metabolically active cells reduce yellow tetrazolium to purple-coloured insoluble formazan which was dissolved using DMSO, whose absorbance was recorded at 570 nm. Penicillin/streptomycin (10,000 U mL−1) and FBS (fetal bovine serum 10% (v/v)) were added to the growth media DMEM (Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium) to get the final media to be used for growing cells. The MTT assay was performed using 96 wells plate and each well containing 100 μL of DMEM was seeded with 1 × 104 cells. The optimum condition for cell growth was attained using an incubator where cells were kept at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere for 24 h. The assay was performed using various concentrations of BCDs in triplicate. The media containing BCDs was replaced after 24 h with fresh media containing MTT (0.5 mg mL−1) and kept in an incubator for 4 h for the formation of purple formazan crystals. DMSO (100 μL) was used to dissolve the insoluble formazan crystals and absorbance was measured at 570 nm. The percent cell viability was calculated using equation no. 4 written below.
(4)
% Cell Viability =
Absorbance
sample
/
Absorbance
Control
× 100

For antibacterial studies, all the labware were autoclaved for 15 min at 121 °C to maintain sterile conditions. Escherichia coli (E.coli DH5α) was cultured in Luria broth at 37 °C under continuous shaking at 120 rpm in an orbital shaker and harvested through centrifugation when the exponential growth phase reached. The optical density of the resuspended bacterial pallet in PBS was measurements at 600 nm (OD600) and the OD was adjusted to 0.1 using PBS to make a fresh inoculum. The 10 μL inoculum was added to 5 mL of fresh Luria broth for performing further experiments. The control was kept under optimal bacterial growth conditions, the other group belonging to the only light group was visible light (400–700 nm) irradiated bacteria for 30 min before incubation, the third group was the bacteria treated with BCDs (200 μg mL−1) and incubated in dark, whereas the fourth group was bacteria treated with BCDs (200 μg mL-1) irradiated using visible light lamp (400–700 nm) for 30 min and then incubated. All the experiments were performed in triplicates. Absorbance was measured at 600 nm after 30 min intervals up to 240 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPBF Assay for Singlet Oxygen (1O2) Measurements
ce:section.ce:para: 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was used for estimating the singlet oxygen (1O2) spawning ability of BCDs [53]. The 1O2 generated after irradiation of BCDs using a visible light (400–700 nm) taken up by DPBF, results in decreased absorbance value [54]. DPBF solution (3 × 10−5 M) was used, into which BCDs were added and the whole mixture was exposed to light. The absorbance was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 414 nm after 5 s light exposure till 150 s.

Hydrothermal carbonization was used for the synthesis of BCDs. The synthesis was performed by heating the whole mixture at 180 °C for 4 h in an autoclave using the furnace. The heteroatom doping was done using different sources like HAuCl4 for gold, AgNO3 for silver, urea, and BSA for nitrogen as shown in Scheme 1 . The quantum yield was calculated and found to be around ~50%. The plausible mechanism behind the formation of BCDs includes carbonization followed by hydrolysis of carbon precursors which can be initiated by increasing temperature followed by dehydration. Further increase in temperature led to condensation hence polymerization which gave rise to carbon nuclei. Rising temperatures lead to the growth of these carbon nuclei within polymeric fragments which ultimately burst and give rise to ultra-small carbon dots [55,56]. The structural and optical characterization was performed to elucidate the insights of prepared BCDs which were further investigated as a picric acid sensor as well as a photoactivated antibacterial agent. It must be mentioned here that nitrogen doping was performed to enhance the fluorescence characteristics of prepared carbon dots owing to its unpaired electron whereas Au and Ag’s doping was incorporated to modulate the overall photosensitizing nature which may lead to higher biocidal nature of as prepared BCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and Structural Characterization of BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To get structural insights of BCDs, XRD, TEM, and FTIR were performed. A broad hump was seen in XRD spectra where the peak lies at 20.23°. The distorted graphite hybridization was indicated by the greater interlayer spacing of 4.3 Å (Fig. 1(a)). The oxygen-rich surface functional groups of carbon dots could be the reason behind distorted sp2 hybridization along with broad peak indicating amorphous nature as seen in XRD [22]. Raman spectroscopy was performed to quantify the sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms present in prepared BCDs. In the present work, BCDs exhibited two prominent Raman bands at 1311.59 cm−1 and 1574.31 cm−1 respectively (Fig.S6.). It can be mentioned here that 785 nm laser sources were used to record the spectra to minimize the fluorescence signal from BCDs [57]. Here the D-band pertains to the defect present in the carbon lattice whereas the G band pertains to the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. Additionally, the higher intensity of the G band in comparison to the D band supports the higher degree of sp2 atoms within the BCDs structure [58,59]. In the FT-IR spectrum of BCDs (Fig. 1(b)), a strong absorbance band is visualized at 3342 cm−1 attributed to the OH stretch of hydroxyl group along with a weak absorbance band at 2093 cm−1 corresponds to OH stretching of carboxy group [60,61]. The carbonyl in the amide bond gives rise to a strong peak of its stretching vibration at 1634 cm-1 [62,63]. A weak absorbance band at 1408 cm−1 attributed to CN stretching vibration [64]. The peaks visualized at, 1224 cm−1 and 618 cm−1 were assigned to C-OH stretch and CH bending respectively [65,66]. The surface passivation with such hydrophilic functional groups helped BCDs to achieve high aqueous solubility along with high stability. TEM micrograph (Fig. 1(d)) showed the size of maximum particles was in the range of 4–8 nm (Fig. 1(c)). The crystallinity of the BCDs was displayed by clear lattice fringes seen in HR-TEM images (Fig. 1(e)), however, the diffused selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 1(f)) indicates the poor crystallinity of the material. From XRD and SAED we can conclude that the BCDs are amorphous particles with some crystallinity as confirmed in many previous reports of carbon dots [67]. In addition to that, a contrast difference was observed between the particles confirming the doping of Au and Ag which was further supported by EDAX results suggesting the presence of Au and Ag in BCDs as mentioned in some of the previous studies [68,69]. ICP-OES analysis was performed to quantify the Au and Ag doping concentration in the as prepared BCDs. The results show the presence of Ag and Au as 11.5272 mg/L and 0.1019 mg/L respectively. Also in the XPS spectra of BCDs, as shown in Fig.S7, the distinguish peaks of the doped elements were found. The data is in line with the previous reports [68–71].

To determine the optical properties of BCDs, UV–Vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy were performed. As shown in figure Fig. 2a (curve Abs) the aqueous solution of BCDs shows a typical absorbance peak at 340 nm. This peak emerges due to n-π* transition at the edge of carbon lattice and occurs because of the energy transfer between carbon band and dopant present within carbon lattice [72]. Such longer wavelength UV peaks generally arise due to the surface functionalization of carbon dots [73]. The bright blue fluorescence coming from a light yellow coloured solution of BCDs could be visualized with naked eyes when irradiated with UV light (365 nm) using a transilluminator (Fig. 2a (inset)). The maximum fluorescence emission was observed at 427 nm (Fig. 2a (Emission)) with excitation maxima at 360 nm (Fig. 2a (Excitation)). Such strong emission is observed when the surface state traps the excited state energy [74]. As illustrated in (Fig. 2b) the BCDs showed excitation-independent emission behaviour. A slight red shift was seen from 427 nm to 433 nm when the excitation wavelength increased from 360 nm to 380 nm, however, at the same time fluorescence intensity was decreased drastically. This behaviour could be explained based on monodispersity as well as surface adsorbed functional groups [75]. Moreover, the uniform crystalline core shown in the HR-TEM image with very few surface layers could also be the reason behind the excitation-independent emission behaviour of BCDs [76]. The average lifetime of BCDs was evaluated as 10.1 ns by using a 375 nm diode laser in TCSPC for which data is fitted to a three-exponential function. This significantly long lifetime makes BCDs the desired candidate to be used as a nanoprobe for fluorescence lifetime imaging in many diagnostic applications [77].

The stability of many fluorescent materials is decisive for determining their applicability in various systems. The stability of BCDs had been investigated at different temperatures, under UV irradiation for 2 h, in NaCl solution to 1 M concentration, and fluorescence stability at room temperature up to 6 months. The results are shown in (Fig. 2(d-f)) revealed the stable nature of BCDs under different conditions. When BCDs solution was irradiated using a 125 W UV lamp at 365 nm for 2 h, up to 90% of initial fluorescence was retained. Similar was the case when the BCDs were suspended in NaCl solution of varying molarity up to 1 M, which enable this sensor to be used in high ionic solutions like ocean water. BCDs are exceptionally stable when exposed to different temperatures. The fluorescence was enhanced a little bit at lower temperatures that are -80 °C and -20 °C while at the highest temperature that is 80 °C the 97% of initial fluorescence was retained. There was not much change in the fluorescence intensity when BCDs are stored at room temperature for six months. The fluorescence was recorded intermittently for this whole period. The stable nature of BCDs makes them a suitable candidate to be used as sensors even in harsh conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Determination of Picric Acid Using BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots have the dual ability to accept electrons as well as donate electrons which is responsible for the quenching of fluorescence owing to the transfer of an electron between analyte and carbon dots. The highly water-soluble BCDs were checked for their sensing response towards nitroaromatic compounds. A strong optical response was observed in the presence of picric acid. The fluorescence of BCDs was “turned off” with the addition of picric acid in the range of 0 μM–500 μM. There is a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity with the addition of picric acid as shown in (Fig. 3c.).

The fluorescence was decreased to 98.72% with the addition of 500 μM of picric acid. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was consistent with a picric acid concentration range from 0 μM–150 μM as depicted in (Fig. 3a.). A very good linear correlation was found between F/F0 and the concentration of picric acid, which is typically described by linear correlation eq. F/F0 = 0.0049 + 0.993[C], where C is the concentration of picric acid as shown in (Fig. 3b.). The correlation coefficient (R2) was found to be 0.99. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated and found to be as low as 46 nM with signal to noise (S/N) ratio of 3. The loss of fluorescence was visible upon serial addition of picric acid under UV transilluminator (365 nm) shown in (Fig. 3d.). The ideal incubation time for picric acid sensing was determined by observing fluorescence spectra of BCDs after the addition of picric acid. After 10 s of incubation, a stable fluorescence was recorded as shown in (Fig. S1.), therefore, a very short time, i.e. 10 s was chosen as incubation time required for picric acid sensing. This observation leads to the conclusion that this method of picric acid sensing using BCDs is expeditious. It is important to mention here that the varying temperature conditions from 15 °C to 70 °C did not have much effect on the sensing ability of BCDs (Fig.S5.). At low temperature (15 °C) there is a 10% increase in fluorescence intensity however, the fluorescence intensity decreases by 15% at high temperature (70 °C) but the relative quenching of fluorescence after the addition of 100 μM of picric acid is the same in all conditions. So, we have chosen room temperature as the optimum temperature for performing all the sensing experiments. Similarly, under the varying pH (1−12) the sensing ability of BCDs has not been hampered and the neutral pH which is the pH of deionized water has been selected as optimal pH for performing all the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of BCDs towards Picric Acid Detection and Sensing Mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed sensor was checked by observing its fluorescence behaviour towards various analytes which are picric acid, 3-nitrotoluene (3-NT), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), aniline, 4-idoaniline (4-IA), nitrobenzene (NB), ortho-nitrophenol (o-NP) 4-nitrotoluene (4-NT), and 3-nitroaniline (3-NA) using the concentration 200 μM of each. It is clear from (Fig. 4a.) that none other than picric acid from the above-mentioned analytes was able to quench fluorescence effectively. The quenching happened due to strong hydrogen bonding and π–π* interaction between the hydroxyl group of picric acid and electron-rich moieties on the surface of BCDs [78]. Moreover, there is a clear overlap between the emission spectrum of BCDs and the absorption spectrum of picric acid (Fig. 4b.), no such overlap was seen with any other analyte's absorption spectra which makes BCDs a selective sensor for picric acid (Fig. S3).

Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was measured using Stern−Volmer (SV) equation no. 5.
(5)
I 0
/ I =
K sv
C
+ 1

I0 represents the fluorescence intensity of BCDs alone, whereas I denote the fluorescence intensity of BCDs after the addition of picric acid. Ksv is quenching constant (M−1) and [C] is the molar concentration of analyte (picric acid). In the SV plot (Fig. S2) it was observed that the graph was linear at lower concentrations of analyte but started to deviate from linearity with increasing analyte concentration. The nonlinear response of the SV plot is suggestive of the energy transfer process or self-absorption between BCDs and picric acid. The direct fitting of the plot was used to calculate the quenching constant (Ksv) which was calculated as 1.358 × 104 M−1.

To further investigate the mechanism behind the “turn-off” behaviour of BCDs in the presence of picric acid, the optical properties of both picric acid as well as BCDs were studied precisely. By looking at the overlapping of absorbance spectra of picric acid and excitation and emission spectra of BCDs, the phenomenon behind fluorescence quenching was expected to be either fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [24]. It is important to mention here that the other nitroaromatic or aromatic compounds used in this study showed a very less irrelevant spectral overlap with BCDs (Fig. S3.). To investigate further TCSPC was performed for the BCDs solution in the presence and absence of picric acid. The average lifetime was reduced from 10.1 ns to 9.94 ns after the addition of picric acid shown in Fig. 5(a). The slight change in the fluorescence lifetime after the addition of picric acid indicates that the quencher is not making any chemical bonding or forming any new complex with the fluorophore. The quencher (picric acid) is binding directly with the fluorophore (BCDs) at the ground state and exhibiting static quenching.

In FRET, the fluorophore and the quencher react chemically to form an intermediate product that is responsible for fluorescence quenching. So, the observation leads to the inclination sensing mechanism towards IFE. The use of fluorophore and quencher as such without the formation of any chemical linking between them makes this sensor more simple and flexible [79,80]. Additionally, when the absorbance spectrum of picric acid after the addition of BCDs was observed, it only shows the enhancement of absorbance value but no new peak was seen. It represents the fact that no new chemical complex is formed after mixing of picric acid and BCDs i.e. they are not reacting chemically. The completely coincided absorption spectrum of picric acid with the excitation spectrum of BCDs which further extended to the emission spectrum of BCDs, also make strong evidence for the self-absorption process i.e. IFE as the primary phenomenon responsible for fluorescence quenching [81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Picric Acid in Tap Water
ce:section.ce:para: The reliability of this analytical sensor for real-time applications was checked using tap water from the laboratory. As expected, no picric acid was detected in tap water using as-synthesized fluorescent probe. Further, water samples were prepared by adding different concentrations of picric acid and a recovery test was performed. The recovery of picric acid was in the range of 93–97% with a standard deviation less than 2 as shown in Table 1 . The very good recovery rate with accuracy and reliability showed the application potential of BCDs sensor to be used in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible Light-Activated Anti-Bacterial Activity of BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the anti-bacterial activity of BCDs, E.coli (DH5a) was used. The optical density measurement method (OD600) was utilized to verify the photo-activated bacterial cell growth inhibition by BCDs. The results shown in (Fig. 6a) revealed the considerable effect of BCDs with light irradiation on E.coli cells. The growth of cells treated with BCDs alone was inhibited in comparison to control cells, however, the light exposure further enhances the antibacterial nature of BCDs confirming the photodynamic antibacterial activity. To find out the use of as prepared antibacterial agent in-vivo, cellular toxicity caused by BCDs was studied using MCF-7 cells. The non-biocompatible nature of BCDs (Fig. S5.) however, renders its application for in-vitro use only. The silver nanoparticles had been explored extensively for their antibacterial activity [82] and doping of antibacterial silver ions plays a role in bacterial killing by BCDs, which get enhanced after photoactivation via the formation of 1O2/ROS. The gold nanoparticles had been used in photodynamic therapies lately [83,84].

The photoinduced bactericidal activity is attributed to doping of BCDs with Au. The carbon dots when absorbing light, electrons jump from the valence band to the conduction band, which leads to the generation of electron-hole pairs as shown in (Fig. 6b). These electrons react with available surface-bound O2 whereas holes as oxidants react with OH− present in the system and lead to the formation of hydroxyl radical [85,86]. The ROS binds with DNA, RNA, cellular proteins and damages the essential components of cells which proves lethal for bacterial growth [85–87]. In addition to that because of the illumination of light, the BCDs damage the bacterial cell structure and deform their bacterial membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Photobiocidal Nature of BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface functionalized carbon dots having very high fluorescence quantum yield were seen to exhibit photo-toxicity to cells [88–91]. The graphene quantum dots were first to be studied for their photo-induced toxic behaviour [88]. A scavenger test using DPBF was performed to check the generation of 1O2 by light irradiation. The 1O2 generated by BCDs upon photoirradiation reacts with DPBF and results in decreased absorbance at 414 nm with each exposure. The data collected after light exposure with 5 s interval were plotted and shown in (Fig. 7a) The periodic decrease in absorbance value inferred photo-induced singlet oxygen generation by BCDs in DCM. This result stipulates that cell death occurred because of ROS produced through photo-activation of BCDs. Furthermore, Au and Ag nanoparticles have already shown enough potential for antibacterial activity thus doping of these elements in carbon dot lattice may produce a more synergistic effect to suppress bacterial growth and improve the overall antibacterial activity of BCDs [92,93]. The results produced here provide lots of potentials to fabricate novel photobiocidal materials to eradicate the transmission of pathogens from different surfaces to different people [85,89,91,94].

In conclusion, the bimetallic doped carbon dots were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesized carbon dots exhibit an excellent quantum yield of 50% with excitation-independent emission behaviour, very good aqueous solubility along with an excellent photo and thermal stability. These carbon dots further act as a superb fluorescent probe for picric acid detection in an aqueous medium. The detection process is simple, selective, and reliable based on IFE and electron transfer between carbon dots and picric acid with a notable LOD 46 nM and quenching constant (KSV) 1.358 × 104 M−1. These carbon dots were also checked for their antibacterial activity against gram-negative E.coli, and results showed the photoactivation of carbon dots with visible light for consequential bactericidal function. So, the present work paves the pathway for use of heteroatom doped carbon dots for comprehensive sensing applications along with their potential use in microbial elimination applications.
